feature: Demo: Spec-driven build of a lightweight team skills directory

issue.title: Demo: Spec-driven build of a lightweight team skills directory

problem_statement: The payroll demo app currently has no way to track or display the skills of individual employees. Adding a Team Skills Directory page will demonstrate the end-to-end OpenHands + OpenSpec spec-driven workflow (spec → plan → tasks → implementation PR) while delivering a small, readable feature that lets users search employees by name, filter by job title, view each employee's skills, and submit skill updates through a simple form.

user_stories.i_want: to view a dedicated Team Skills Directory page listing all employees with their skills
user_stories.so_that: I can quickly understand the capabilities available across the team

user_stories.i_want: to search employees by name on the skills directory page
user_stories.so_that: I can quickly locate a specific person's skill profile

user_stories.i_want: to filter employees by job title on the skills directory page
user_stories.so_that: I can see the skills held by people in a particular role

user_stories.i_want: to submit a form to add or update skills for an employee
user_stories.so_that: I can keep skill records accurate as employees grow their capabilities

user_stories.i_want: to see the full spec → plan → tasks → PR workflow on a real, self-contained feature
user_stories.so_that: I can evaluate whether the OpenSpec process works end-to-end

must_have: A new /skills route serving a Team Skills Directory page
The page lists all employees with their name, job title, and current skills
Skills are stored per employee in a new SQLite table linked to the existing employees table
A search input on the page filters the employee list by name (server-side or client-side)
A dropdown or select input on the page filters employees by job title
A form to add or replace the skill list for a selected employee (comma-separated or line-by-line input is acceptable)
The directory page is navigable from the main payroll dashboard (a link in the nav or header)
Input is validated: employee must exist, skills field must not be empty on submit
should_have: Flash/redirect messages on successful skill update (consistent with existing redirect-with-message pattern)
Empty-state message when no employees match the active search/filter
Skills displayed as readable tags or a comma-separated list rather than raw JSON
The feature branch, spec, plan, tasks, and PR artifacts are all linked in issue comments to demonstrate workflow transparency
nice_to_have: Client-side live filtering without a full page reload
Ability to delete individual skills rather than replacing the entire skill list
Skill count summary (e.g., total distinct skills across the team)

acceptance_criteria: GET /skills returns a 200 HTML response listing all employees with their name, title, and skills
A navigation link to /skills is visible on the main dashboard page
Searching by a partial employee name on the skills page shows only matching employees
Filtering by a job title on the skills page shows only employees with that title
POST /employees/:id/skills with a valid skill list updates the employee's skills and redirects with a success message
POST /employees/:id/skills with a missing or empty skill list returns an error message without updating the database
POST /employees/:id/skills for a non-existent employee ID returns an error message
Skills are persisted in SQLite and visible on page reload
The spec.json artifact exists and passes OpenSpec schema validation
Plan, tasks, and implementation PR artifacts are all generated and linked in the issue comments
out_of_scope: Authentication or role-based access control for the skills page
Skill categories, proficiency levels, or endorsements
Bulk import or export of skills (e.g., CSV upload)
Real-time collaboration or concurrent edit handling
Integration with external skill or HR systems
Mobile-optimized or accessible design beyond basic HTML
open_questions: Should skills be stored as a free-text field (comma-separated string) or as individual rows in a junction table? A single text field is simpler for a demo; a junction table is more extensible.
Should the search and title filter be applied server-side (query params) or client-side (JavaScript)? Client-side is simpler but server-side is more consistent with the existing app pattern.
Should the skills form replace all existing skills for an employee, or append new ones? Replacing is simpler for a demo scope.
Is there a preferred visual style or component pattern (e.g., matching the existing payroll dashboard CSS) that should be followed for the new page?
